column2: Lilichka and Miyechka sat on the grass.
The grandmother stood leaning on her cane, and with it pointed out which dress to keep.
Marta grimaced at every dress; they all roused in her a mixture of squeamishness and irritation.
After a general inspection of the bag's contents Marta finally said:
— I'll go and buy her something.
— Tires first, — said Lilichka.
— Fine, — retorted Marta, took the child by the hand and walked slowly with her toward the house, saying that they were going to have breakfast.
— Would you look at that, — the grandmother said to Miyechka.
— And you said Marta's maternal instinct wouldn't wake up.
And look how it is.
We have a girl
As soon as the car doors shut and the two of them were alone, locked in the red Honda, and the voices of the others, including the morning birds, fell silent, Miyechka asked at once:
— Well, what was it you wanted to tell me?
— Ivan isn't picking up, — said Lilichka and started the car.
— Not at all?
— He's vanished, — retorted Lilichka and, throwing her arm over the next seat, the way the grandfather always used to, began backing out through the gate.
— And Natalya?
— Which Natalya?
— His wife.
— I don't have her number.
And I have no other numbers, only Ivan's, — said Lilichka, visibly panicking.
— Not bad, — retorted Miyechka, once they had already pulled out onto the road.
— Not bad, — she repeated.
— We have a girl.

They left the car on the shoulder of the village, through which an intercity highway tore, knocking the local cats dead and the alcoholics off their reckoning: the smarter of them would lie down for the night on the other side, not risking crossing at night, while the others risked it, but not all of them survived.
On one side there was an auto repair shop.
On the shoulder stood a large yellowed piece of cardboard, on which in fat red strokes was written: 'Tire Service.'
Two men worked at the repair shop.
One of middling years and middling wits, always ready to chat up the girls.
The other — young, handsome and very quiet.
Lilichka and Miyechka parked by the sign.
Lilichka wore denim shorts and a khaki-colored tank top.
She held one hand to her shoulder, covering the neckline.
Miyechka glanced at her sister and told her to wait.
Then she climbed into the car, trying to find something under the tires, two of which lay in the cabin.
— What are you doing?
— asked Lilichka.
— I'm looking for a scarf for you to cover up with, — Miyechka shouted from somewhere deep in the cabin.

— You're searching for that scarf so invitingly that very soon the search will lose all its point, — said Miyechka, glancing at how her sister had climbed with one foot onto the seat, and at the mechanic, who had already come out past the gates of the shop.
— Need a hand, girls?
— he shouted.
Lilichka positioned herself so as to screen Miyechka's pose, and shouted:
— In a moment!
— Mia, get into the car already, let me drive into their yard.
Solomiya slammed the door behind her and cried out:
— Found it!
— Lord, don't startle me, — retorted Lilichka, having started the car.
— Hand over your scarf.
From the back seat Miyechka held out to her sister a sky-blue scarf with a very fine little checked pattern.
She wound it around her neck so that its ends lay loosely over her chest.
— As if there were anything to cover up, — she added.
In the booth where the mechanics kept their tools hung a poster of an almost naked girl who, with her legs spread, leaned against a car hood.
Beneath the poster sat the pensive young man — the younger one.
The poster strangely matched the sky-blue innocent eyes of the tire-fitter.
Lilichka, wrapped up in the scarf, asked about the price.
— Ask my father, — said the young man.
— So that's your father?
— asked Lilichka.
The young man shrugged one shoulder and smiled.
Then, evidently, he remembered that he was sitting under a poster of a naked woman, and grew embarrassed.
— We'll leave the car and take a walk ourselves, — said Miyechka.
— But write down a phone number.
— But where is there to walk around here?
— asked the young man's father, who had come into the booth.
— You should stay here with us!
— Write down a number, — Miyechka repeated.
The young man picked up a squared notebook, opened it and began leafing through, looking for the page with phone numbers.
His hands were dirty and large.
He took a blue ballpoint pen in his left hand and carefully wrote out: '+38.'
Miyechka came up to the table, covered with a greasy oilcloth with large red flowers, and dictated the number, leaning over the table.
The young man diligently wrote out the large digits in the notebook with his left hand.
— Hot, — said Lilichka, when she and her sister had come out of the booth.

— That's how you begin to understand all those noblewomen who used to slip off into the bushes with their serfs, leaving on their own their rosy-cheeked, pale-faced husbands with shirt-fronts propping up their double chins… — she said dreamily.
The sky-blue scarf fluttered in the wind.
— And did I tell you, — Miyechka began, — that Danylo is always perfectly clean and smells of soap?
— Smells of soap?
And does he have a smell of his own?
— No, it's as if he doesn't smell of anything at all.
Only of soap.
— Or maybe he — remember how in childhood grandmother used to put bars of soap between the sheets?
— so maybe he's like that too?
— He doesn't allow me to look in his wardrobe.
I even thought once that he was hiding something there, and, when he was out, I looked.
— And?
Love notes?
Drugs?
Porn magazines?
Cash?
— Lilichka generated ideas.
— Nothing.
He simply found it disgusting that I would touch his laundered shirts with my hands.
— And was there soap?
— There wasn't.
We don't have soap with that smell at home.
It's as if it seeps out from under his skin.
— Maybe his mother soaped him up uncontrollably in childhood, so much that she formed a subcutaneous reserve?
The sisters came up to the road which, in the dark hours and especially on Fridays and Sundays, when people migrated from the city to the villages and back, divided the village into two banks.
It was Wednesday, and, holding hands, they managed to dash quickly across to the other bank.
And on that side there was the café 'Lidiya.'
They sat down under a heavy velvet curtain, the kind that usually hangs in minibuses too and slaps you in the face if the window is open.
— What can I get you, — a young girl shouted from behind the counter.
She wore a maroon top with a deep, full neckline.
She had no intention of coming out.
— I'll run over, — said Miyechka.
Lilichka sat by the window and watched the enormous trucks tearing past the café windows in both directions.
Her sister brought two mugs, in which two tea bags dangled limply in moderately hot water.
— Chamomile, — said Miyechka.
— Let me warm my hands, it's such a cold day today, — retorted Lilichka and went on: — So then, we have a girl.
— Listen, Lilya, — Solomiya answered, — you know that you don't owe anyone anything.
— Mia, it's a child.
And not just any child.
It's Lusya.
This particular Lusya, with her eyes and those helpless little braids, — Lilichka lifted her own hair with her fingers.
It just stayed sticking up.
Her hair was like a doll's: it didn't fall loose, it just lay there.
— You don't have to become a mother to her.
— I know I don't have to, but she's the half-sister of my son, when you think about it.
And what if no one ever takes her away from me?
— Listen, this summer… Well, you know what kind of summer this is.
This is a pause-summer.
We'll decide everything after the summer, and for now let Lusya just be Lusya.
— And you?
And what are you going to do?
With all this soap?
And that other one, how does he smell?
— He smells of himself.
I'm telling you, this is a pause-summer.
Maybe it's the last summer like this.
You know what I mean…
— I know, — said Lilichka, holding the mug with both palms.
— Ninety-six years old.
— Ninety-six, Lilya, — repeated Miyechka.
On the way back Miyechka was at the wheel.
You could see her bare legs with dark marks from the tires they had loaded in the morning.
— We'll be there soon and won't be able to talk in private, — she said.
— Then we'll go and change the tires back to winter ones again, — Lilichka laughed.
— We'll have lukewarm chamomile tea again from that girl.
Did you see her breasts?
— Yep.
— Impressive, no?
— How do you live with ones like that?
— How? Well —
You wake up in the morning and already feel that you're not living in vain.
— So they give you a fullness of life.
— A physical fullness of life.
— Well, big breasts, — Miyechka began, — actually few people really like them…
— Su-u-ure, — Lilichka laughed.
— Who needs them anyway?
Poor busty women!
— Poor things, — said Miyechka and peered sadly down the collar of her T-shirt.
Lilichka automatically took hold of the wheel with one hand.
And then cried out:
— Brake!
Alongside the road, beside the tree belt, a man was walking with a bag.
He wore a checked, lined dark-blue-and-white shirt, dark trousers, dress shoes and a flat cap.
Miyechka slowed down, stopped and opened the window, calling to the man to get into the car with them.
He briskly snatched up his bag, looked both ways down the empty road and ran across to their side.
— And where are you off to?
— began Miyechka, who liked to make chitchat with travelers.
— And whose are you?
— the man asked at once.
— Oh, we're not from around here, — said Miyechka, flashing a glance at him in the mirror.
The man had deep-set blue eyes under shaggy brows.
— So where are you going, then?
— he pressed.
— We're going to our grandmother's.
— So whose are you, then?
— Well, the man who planted the apple orchard, you know?
— I don't.
— The one who planted the spruces in the grove by the river, you know?
— A-ha-ha, — the traveler went on going 'aha.'
— I know, I know, he was a good man, though he paid everyone little.
— But that was done for the community, — Lilichka cut in.
— For whom?
— For everyone, I'm saying, it was done.
The spruces just grow, after all.
— They grow so well that people came all the way from the city to shoot a film here at New Year, — said the man.
— A hundred or so of them came, and half of them naked.
And in the dead of winter, mind you.
— So what kind of film was it?
— asked Miyechka.
— They said it was a love film, since everything was heading that way, everyone naked and all, and then it turned out to be a commercial.
— And what were they advertising?
— Some kind of champagne, — said the man.
He took hold of the front seat with his hand and shouted into the windshield.
— So what are you to that man who planted the spruces?
Are you his daughters?
— Granddaughters.
The man fell quiet, Miyechka turned on the radio.
The car raced merrily down the empty road.
